1→/*
     2→ * parity.h - Dual-execution parity testing for OCaml bridge
     3→ *
     4→ * Snapshots full game state before/after OCaml and C execution paths,
     5→ * compares field-by-field, and logs divergences. C path is canonical.
     6→ *
     7→ * Compile with USE_OX_BRIDGE to enable. Zero overhead when disabled.
     8→ *
     9→ * Runtime control:
    10→ *   OX_PARITY_ASSERT=1  - crash on first divergence
    11→ *   OX_PARITY_LOG=file  - write divergences to file
    12→ */
    13→
    14→#ifndef OX_PARITY_H
    15→#define OX_PARITY_H
    16→
    17→#ifdef USE_OX_BRIDGE
    18→
    19→#ifdef __cplusplus
    20→extern "C" {
    21→#endif
    22→
    23→#include "fix/fix.h"
    24→
    25→/* Forward declarations - actual types come from game headers */
    26→struct object;
    27→struct ai_local;
    28→struct player;
    29→struct wall;
    30→
    31→#define PARITY_MAX_OBJECTS  350
    32→#define PARITY_MAX_PLAYERS  8
    33→#define PARITY_MAX_WALLS    254
    34→#define PARITY_MAX_SEGMENTS 900
    35→
    36→typedef struct game_state_snapshot {
    37→	/* Object array - only up to Highest_object_index+1 are meaningful */
    38→	struct object   objects[PARITY_MAX_OBJECTS];
    39→	struct ai_local ai_local_info[PARITY_MAX_OBJECTS];
    40→	struct player   players[PARITY_MAX_PLAYERS];
    41→	struct wall     walls[PARITY_MAX_WALLS];
    42→
    43→	/* Per-segment object list heads (obj_relink modifies these) */
    44→	short           seg_objects[PARITY_MAX_SEGMENTS];
    45→	int             highest_segment_index;
    46→
    47→	/* Key globals */
    48→	unsigned int    rand_state;
    49→	int             highest_object_index;
    50→	int             player_is_dead;
    51→	int             player_exploded;
    52→	int             overall_agitation;
    53→	int             num_walls;
    54→	fix             frame_time;
    55→	fix             game_time;
    56→	int             frame_count;
    57→} game_state_snapshot;
    58→
    59→/*
    60→ * Snapshot the current game state into dst.
    61→ */
    62→void parity_snapshot(game_state_snapshot *dst);
    63→
    64→/*
    65→ * Restore game state from src (used between OCaml and C runs).
    66→ */
    67→void parity_restore(const game_state_snapshot *src);
    68→
    69→/*
    70→ * Compare two snapshots field-by-field.
    71→ * Logs all divergences to stderr (and optionally to file).
    72→ * Returns number of divergent fields.
    73→ *
    74→ * fn_name: which function was being compared (e.g. "do_physics_sim")
    75→ * frame: current frame number
    76→ * objnum: object being processed (-1 if N/A)
    77→ */
    78→int parity_compare(const game_state_snapshot *a, const game_state_snapshot *b,
    79→                   const char *fn_name, int frame, int objnum);
    80→
    81→/*
    82→ * Initialize parity system. Call once at startup.
    83→ * Reads OX_PARITY_ASSERT and OX_PARITY_LOG environment variables.
    84→ */
    85→void parity_init(void);
    86→
    87→/*
    88→ * Pre-allocated snapshot buffers for use in dual-execution wrappers.
    89→ * Avoids repeated allocation of ~350KB snapshots.
    90→ */
    91→extern game_state_snapshot parity_snap_before;
    92→extern game_state_snapshot parity_snap_after_ocaml;
    93→extern game_state_snapshot parity_snap_after_c;
    94→
    95→#ifdef __cplusplus
    96→}
    97→#endif
    98→
    99→#endif /* USE_OX_BRIDGE */
   100→
   101→#endif /* OX_PARITY_H */
   102→

file.content:
let start_pos_x = pos_x in
    let start_pos_y = pos_y in
    let start_pos_z = pos_z in
    let orig_segnum = obj_segnum in
    let obj_stopped = ref false in
    let retry_count = ref 0 in
    let n_ignore_objs = ref 0 in
    let ignore_objs = Array.create ~len:max_ignore_objs (-1) in
    let n_phys_segs = ref 0 in
    let phys_seglist = Array.create ~len:max_fvi_segs 0 in
    let try_again = ref true in
    let early_return = ref false in
    ignore phys_seglist;
    (* seglist is tracked but returned via n_phys_segs count *)
    while !try_again && not !early_return do
      try_again := false;
      (* frame_vec = velocity * sim_time *)
      let fvx, fvy, fvz =
        Ox_math.vm_vec_copy_scale ~v:(!cur_vel_x, !cur_vel_y, !cur_vel_z) ~k:!sim_time
      in
      if fvx = 0 && fvy = 0 && fvz = 0
      then () (* break *)
      else (
        retry_count := !retry_count + 1;
        if !retry_count > 3 && not (obj_type = obj_player && !retry_count <= 8)
        then () (* break: too many retries *)
        else (
        let new_px, new_py, new_pz =
          Ox_math.vm_vec_add ~a:(!cur_pos_x, !cur_pos_y, !cur_pos_z) ~b:(fvx, fvy, fvz)
        in
        let ignore_list = make_ignore_list ignore_objs !n_ignore_objs in
        let fq_flags = ref fq_check_objs in
        if obj_type = obj_weapon then fq_flags := !fq_flags lor fq_transpoint;
        if obj_type = obj_player then fq_flags := !fq_flags lor fq_get_seglist;
        let ( fate
            , hit_px
            , hit_py
            , hit_pz
            , hit_seg
            , hit_side
            , hit_side_seg
            , hit_objnum
            , wn_x
            , wn_y
            , wn_z
            , n_segs
            , seglist )
          =
          Effect.perform
            (Find_vector_intersection
               ( !cur_pos_x
               , !cur_pos_y
               , !cur_pos_z
               , new_px
               , new_py
               , new_pz
               , size
               , objnum
               , ignore_list
               , !fq_flags
               , !cur_segnum ))
        in
        (* D1: proximity mine hack — don't count hitting a proximity mine as a retry *)
        if fate = hit_object && hit_objnum >= 0
        then (
          let (ho_type, ho_id) = Effect.perform (Query_object_type_id hit_objnum) in
          if ho_type = obj_weapon && ho_id = proximity_id
          then retry_count := !retry_count - 1);
        (* Player seglist tracking *)
        if obj_type = obj_player
        then (
          if
            !n_phys_segs > 0
            && n_segs > 0
            && phys_seglist.(!n_phys_segs - 1) = seglist.(0)
          then n_phys_segs := !n_phys_segs - 1;
          let i = ref 0 in
          while !i < n_segs && !n_phys_segs < max_fvi_segs - 1 do
            phys_seglist.(!n_phys_segs) <- seglist.(!i);
            n_phys_segs := !n_phys_segs + 1;

OK so OCaml returns `retry_count - 1` when `retry_count > 0`, just like C. But there's an important difference:

In C (physics.cpp line 1450-1457):
```c
if (obj->control_type == CT_AI) {
    if (count > 0) {
        Ai_local_info[objnum].retry_count = count-1;
    }
}
```
Note: C's `count` starts at 0 and is incremented at the top of the loop. So after one iteration with no retry, `count = 1`, and retry_count = count-1 = 0.


In OCaml, `retry_count` starts at 0 and gets incremented at the beginning of each loop iteration, making it equivalent to C's `count` variable. When returned as `retry_count - 1` (when greater than 0), it produces the same result as C. Now I need to check how the bridge writes this value back in physics.cpp to see if there's a mismatch...

The bridge only writes the result back if it's greater than 0, which means a single collision (where OCaml returns 0) won't get written back to C, preserving whatever value was there before. This is different from C's behavior where `count - 1` would be 0 and wouldn't pass the `if count > 0` check either, so actually both paths skip writing when there's no retry or just one collision.

But the actual divergence I'm seeing suggests the OCaml physics simulation itself is producing different retry counts than C, likely cascading from earlier FVI differences rather than from the write-back logic itself.

Looking at the actual divergence pattern, the first mismatch appears at frame 16 in ai_local[55]'s retry_count field, which is quite early. I should examine what's driving the largest divergences across the dataset rather than just chasing the first one—like the frame 43 object[92] divergences where position and orientation bytes differ significantly. The retry_count divergence seems isolated to just that one frame, while consecutive_retries shows a pattern across frames 17-24. B=1 — persists

This suggests that in OCaml, object 55 had one collision retry at frame 16 (retry_count set to 1), which got accumulated into consecutive_retries at frame 17+. In C, the object had no retry (retry_count stayed 0).

Wait actually, let me re-read: retry_count is at byte 4-7 (int, 4 bytes). byte 4 would be the first byte of this int. On little-endian (arm64), byte 4 = low byte. So A=0x00 means the low byte of C's retry_count is 0 (so retry_count=0), and B=0x01 means the low byte of OCaml's retry_count is 1 (so retry_count=1).

But result[22] is `retry_count - 1`.

So if OCaml's retry_count was 2, it returns 1 to the bridge, while C had retry_count=0 or 1. This points to an extra collision in OCaml's FVI or physics simulation at frame 16 — likely a divergence from an unported sub-function rather than a logic bug. But with 650,526 divergences across 6796 frames, I need to step back and see if there's a systemic issue here.

The divergence rate is steady at 9572 per 100 frames, matching the baseline. The ai_local divergences dominate at 384,783, followed by object divergences at 247,790. Given that FVI and physics are OCaml-authoritative, any unported sub-function would cascade divergences. The object[92] position/orientation divergences starting at frame 43 suggest a major positional shift — likely stemming from an unported function affecting physics or collision handling.

Looking at the ai.cpp retry_count logic around line 969, when consecutive_retries exceeds 3, it triggers path creation and mode changes that would cascade through all downstream AI behavior. If the retry_count differs between implementations, everything downstream diverges. But tracing that back to the source would require diving into collision code.

Let me focus on what function port would have the highest impact. The SUMMARY section shows time_since_processed diverging at frame 6796 for ai_local[131], which suggests state tracking is drifting across frames. I'm looking at the divergence data and seeing timing and state differences that suggest some events are happening in different orders between the two versions. Rather than chasing these low-level mismatches, I should focus on porting an unported C function to pure OCaml—specifically one that's currently called as an effect through the bridge. content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile my findings into a clear report:

## Summary: Algebraic Effects in ox/ox_ai_frame.ml

Based on my analysis of the ChocolateDescent codebase, here's a complete inventory of **all algebraic effects** performed in `ox/ox_ai_frame.ml` that call back into C via the bridge. There are **23 distinct effect types** (26 if counting D2-only variants).

---

### EFFECTS PERFORMED IN ox_ai_frame.ml

| Effect | Call Count | Type Category | Portability Analysis |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-----------------------|
| **Ai_multiplayer_awareness** | ~50+ | Network/Game State | **MODERATE**: Checks if multiplayer agitation should trigger awareness in player. Currently calls C for multiplayer check. *Pure logic candidate if multiplayer flag and agitation thresholds are available.* |
| **Do_ai_robot_hit_attack** | 3 | Combat/Game State | **HIGH**: Likely just sets flags on robot/player. *Good candidate for pure OCaml.* |
| **Ai_fire_laser_at_player** | ~5 | Combat/Side Effect | **LOW**: Creates actual laser objects; must stay in C. *Requires C for object creation effect.* |
| **Calc_gun_point** | ~5 | Calculation | **VERY HIGH**: Pure geometry; computes gun firing position from robot to player. *Excellent candidate—just 3D vector math on fixed-point coordinates.* |
| **Create_path_to_player** | ~10 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Full pathfinding algorithm with segment traversal. *Needs complex C world data (visibility, segment connectivity).* |
| **Create_path_to_station** | ~5 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Similar to above; station-based pathfinding. |
| **Create_n_segment_path** | ~15 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Random N-segment path generation; needs segment/wall data from C. |
| **Create_n_segment_path_to_door** | ~10 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Like above, scans for doors in segments. |
| **Attempt_to_resume_path** | 4 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Resumes existing path; needs C path state. |
| **Ai_follow_path** | ~5 | Pathfinding/Movement | **MODERATE**: Advances along existing path. *Could be ported if path array format is known, but requires segment validity checks.* |
| **Move_towards_segment_center** | 6 | Movement | **HIGH**: Moves robot toward center of current segment. *Pure logic if segment center is computed in advance.* |
| **Compute_vis_and_vec** | ~5 | Visibility/Perception | **VERY LOW**: Full FVI (Find Vector Intersection) via effect callbacks. *Requires on-demand world data fetching; architectural blocker.* |
| **Ai_multi_send_robot_position** | ~40+ | Network/Multiplayer | **LOW**: Network broadcast; must stay in C. |
| **Do_boss_stuff** | 2 | Boss-specific AI | **MODERATE**: Boss-specific state updates. *Potentially portable if logic is isolated.* |
| **P_Rand** | 16 | Randomness | **VERY HIGH**: Simple random number. *Trivial to port—just call OCaml's Random module.* |
| **Make_random_vector** | ~5 | Randomness/Geometry | **HIGH**: Generates random 3D vector. *Portable; just 3D random math.* |
| **Object_to_object_visibility** | 0 | Visibility | **LOW**: Query if two objects can see each other; blocked by FVI complexity. |
| **Do_snipe_frame** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 sniper robot AI frame; full C implementation. |
| **Do_escort_frame** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 escort robot AI frame; full C implementation. |
| **Do_thief_frame** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 thief robot AI frame; full C implementation. |
| **Do_any_robot_dying_frame** | 1 | Combat/Death | **MODERATE**: Robot death animation/state. *Potentially portable.* |
| **Make_nearby_robot_snipe** | 1 | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2-only; initiates snipe mode on nearby robots. |
| **Move_away_from_player** | 2 | Movement | **MODERATE**: Moves robot away from player. *Portable if uses local position/velocity without needing world queries.* |
| **Openable_doors_in_segment** | 1 | World Query | **LOW**: Queries segment for doors; needs C world data. |
| **Invalidate_escort_goal** | 1 | D2 AI State | **MODERATE**: Resets escort target; likely flag-setting. |
| **Laser_create_new_easy** | 0+ | Combat/Side Effect | **LOW**: Creates laser object; must stay in C. |
| **Do_companion_extras** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 companion robot laser evasion + flare firing. |
| **Do_thief_extras** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 thief robot flare firing. |

---

### HIGH-PRIORITY PORTING CANDIDATES (Reduce ai_local divergence)

Ranked by:
1. **Pure logic** (no world state queries)
2. **High call frequency** (reduce effect overhead)
3. **AI state impact** (directly changes ai_local fields)

#### **🟢 Tier 1: Very High Priority (Pure Math)**

1. **`P_Rand`** (16 calls/frame)
   - **What**: Returns random int
   - **Why**: Trivial; replace C `d_rand()` with OCaml `Random.int`
   - **Impact**: Reduces random-seeded divergences across 16+ call sites
   - **Porting effort**: Minimal (5 lines)

2. **`Calc_gun_point`** (~5 calls)
   - **What**: Computes firing position from gun mount relative to robot orientation
   - **Pure inputs**: Gun number, robot position, orientation (all provided)
   - **Why**: Pure 3D vector math on fixed-point integers
   - **Impact**: Eliminates divergence in gun point calculations (affects firing direction)
   - **Porting effort**: Low (~20 lines of vector math)

3. **`Make_random_vector`** (~5 calls)
   - **What**: Generates random normalized 3D vector
   - **Why**: Pure randomness; no world data needed
   - **Impact**: Used in AI decision branching; eliminating this effect would consolidate random paths
   - **Porting effort**: Low (~15 lines)

#### **🟡 Tier 2: Medium Priority (Potential Pure Logic)**

4. **`Move_towards_segment_center`** (6 calls)
   - **What**: Moves robot toward center of current segment
   - **Need**: Current segment geometry (provided in inputs)
   - **Why**: Likely pure vector subtraction/normalization
   - **Impact**: Reduces divergence in robot movement tracking
   - **Porting effort**: Medium (~30 lines)

5. **`Do_boss_stuff`** (2 calls)
   - **What**: Boss-specific state updates (teleporting, summons, etc.)
   - **Why**: May be isolated logic with limited side effects
   - **Impact**: Fixes boss AI behavior divergences
   - **Porting effort**: High (varies by boss implementation)

6. **`Do_ai_robot_hit_attack`** (3 calls)
   - **What**: Robot close-range collision attack (melee)
   - **Why**: Likely flag-setting or damage calculation
   - **Impact**: Fixes collision-based attack divergence
   - **Porting effort**: Medium (~15 lines)

#### **🔴 Tier 3: Low Priority (Architectural Blockers)**

7. **`Compute_vis_and_vec`** (~5 calls)
   - **Blocker**: Requires full FVI (Find Vector Intersection) via on-demand effects
   - **Current design**: Uses 3x effect callbacks (Fetch_segment_data, Fetch_object_data, Fetch_collision_table) per visibility check
   - **Inefficiency**: Shadow mode doubles computation (C runs FVI, then OCaml runs FVI via callbacks)
   - **Porting note**: FVI v2 is already ported; integrate it directly instead of effect-based
   - **Impact**: Would eliminate ~37-57 second headless replay slowdown

8. **Path creation effects** (`Create_path_to_player`, `Create_n_segment_path`, etc.)
   - **Blocker**: Full pathfinding requires traversing segment graph + obstacle avoidance
   - **Why**: Needs mutable world state (visited sets, path arrays) and segment connectivity
   - **Porting effort**: Very High (100+ lines per function)

9. **Multiplayer effects** (`Ai_multiplayer_awareness`, `Ai_multi_send_robot_position`)
   - **Why**: Network/game mode dependent; side effects on global state
   - **Porting effort**: High (complex game mode logic)

---

### RECOMMENDED PORTING SEQUENCE

To maximize reduction in `ai_local` divergence and improve headless replay performance:

1. **Start with `P_Rand` + `Make_random_vector`** (Week 1)
   - Replace C RNG calls; consolidates random branches
   - Test: Verify AI behavior determinism improves

2. **Port `Calc_gun_point`** (Week 1-2)
   - Pure 3D math
   - Test: Verify firing direction matches C across all robot types

3. **Port `Move_towards_segment_center`** (Week 2)
   - Validates segment position availability in inputs

4. **Consider full `Compute_vis_and_vec` integration** (Week 3+)
   - Merge FVI v2 directly into do_ai_frame instead of effect callbacks
   - Eliminates major shadow-mode performance bottleneck

---

### CURRENT EFFECT CALLSITES IN ox_ai_frame.ml

The file is split into three main functions:

- **`ai_do_actual_firing_stuff_d1`** (lines ~290-388): ~13 effects for D1 fire logic
- **`ai_do_actual_firing_stuff_d2`** (lines ~440-600): ~20 effects for D2 fire logic (with dual-gun, sniper, thief)
- **`do_ai_frame_d1`** (lines ~650-1500+): ~100 effects for full D1 AI frame
- **`do_ai_frame_d2`** (lines ~1700-2500+): ~160 effects for full D2 AI frame

The bridge adapter in `ai_frame_bridge.ml` (514 lines) handles all 23+ effects via `Effect.Deep.match_with`, dispatching to C externals and unpacking returned state.

---

### KEY OBSERVATION

**The biggest gains come from eliminating randomness-seeded divergences.** Currently, 16+ `P_Rand` calls per frame in do_ai_frame mean OCaml and C can follow completely different random paths after the first divergence. Porting `P_Rand` to use a shared PRNG state (or OCaml-authoritative randomness) would dramatically improve parity.

In the ChocolateDescent project, I need to find which algebraic effects are performed in ox/ox_ai_frame.ml that call back into C (via the bridge), and are candidates for porting to pure OCaml. 

Specifically:
1. List ALL `Effect.perform` calls in ox/ox_ai_frame.ml with their effect names

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile my findings into a clear report:

## Summary: Algebraic Effects in ox/ox_ai_frame.ml

Based on my analysis of the ChocolateDescent codebase, here's a complete inventory of **all algebraic effects** performed in `ox/ox_ai_frame.ml` that call back into C via the bridge. There are **23 distinct effect types** (26 if counting D2-only variants).

---

### EFFECTS PERFORMED IN ox_ai_frame.ml

| Effect | Call Count | Type Category | Portability Analysis |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-----------------------|
| **Ai_multiplayer_awareness** | ~50+ | Network/Game State | **MODERATE**: Checks if multiplayer agitation should trigger awareness in player. Currently calls C for multiplayer check. *Pure logic candidate if multiplayer flag and agitation thresholds are available.* |
| **Do_ai_robot_hit_attack** | 3 | Combat/Game State | **HIGH**: Likely just sets flags on robot/player. *Good candidate for pure OCaml.* |
| **Ai_fire_laser_at_player** | ~5 | Combat/Side Effect | **LOW**: Creates actual laser objects; must stay in C. *Requires C for object creation effect.* |
| **Calc_gun_point** | ~5 | Calculation | **VERY HIGH**: Pure geometry; computes gun firing position from robot to player. *Excellent candidate—just 3D vector math on fixed-point coordinates.* |
| **Create_path_to_player** | ~10 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Full pathfinding algorithm with segment traversal. *Needs complex C world data (visibility, segment connectivity).* |
| **Create_path_to_station** | ~5 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Similar to above; station-based pathfinding. |
| **Create_n_segment_path** | ~15 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Random N-segment path generation; needs segment/wall data from C. |
| **Create_n_segment_path_to_door** | ~10 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Like above, scans for doors in segments. |
| **Attempt_to_resume_path** | 4 | Pathfinding | **LOW**: Resumes existing path; needs C path state. |
| **Ai_follow_path** | ~5 | Pathfinding/Movement | **MODERATE**: Advances along existing path. *Could be ported if path array format is known, but requires segment validity checks.* |
| **Move_towards_segment_center** | 6 | Movement | **HIGH**: Moves robot toward center of current segment. *Pure logic if segment center is computed in advance.* |
| **Compute_vis_and_vec** | ~5 | Visibility/Perception | **VERY LOW**: Full FVI (Find Vector Intersection) via effect callbacks. *Requires on-demand world data fetching; architectural blocker.* |
| **Ai_multi_send_robot_position** | ~40+ | Network/Multiplayer | **LOW**: Network broadcast; must stay in C. |
| **Do_boss_stuff** | 2 | Boss-specific AI | **MODERATE**: Boss-specific state updates. *Potentially portable if logic is isolated.* |
| **P_Rand** | 16 | Randomness | **VERY HIGH**: Simple random number. *Trivial to port—just call OCaml's Random module.* |
| **Make_random_vector** | ~5 | Randomness/Geometry | **HIGH**: Generates random 3D vector. *Portable; just 3D random math.* |
| **Object_to_object_visibility** | 0 | Visibility | **LOW**: Query if two objects can see each other; blocked by FVI complexity. |
| **Do_snipe_frame** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 sniper robot AI frame; full C implementation. |
| **Do_escort_frame** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 escort robot AI frame; full C implementation. |
| **Do_thief_frame** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 thief robot AI frame; full C implementation. |
| **Do_any_robot_dying_frame** | 1 | Combat/Death | **MODERATE**: Robot death animation/state. *Potentially portable.* |
| **Make_nearby_robot_snipe** | 1 | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2-only; initiates snipe mode on nearby robots. |
| **Move_away_from_player** | 2 | Movement | **MODERATE**: Moves robot away from player. *Portable if uses local position/velocity without needing world queries.* |
| **Openable_doors_in_segment** | 1 | World Query | **LOW**: Queries segment for doors; needs C world data. |
| **Invalidate_escort_goal** | 1 | D2 AI State | **MODERATE**: Resets escort target; likely flag-setting. |
| **Laser_create_new_easy** | 0+ | Combat/Side Effect | **LOW**: Creates laser object; must stay in C. |
| **Do_companion_extras** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 companion robot laser evasion + flare firing. |
| **Do_thief_extras** (D2 only) | 0+ | D2 AI Behavior | **LOW**: D2 thief robot flare firing. |

---

### HIGH-PRIORITY PORTING CANDIDATES (Reduce ai_local divergence)

Ranked by:
1. **Pure logic** (no world state queries)
2. **High call frequency** (reduce effect overhead)
3. **AI state impact** (directly changes ai_local fields)

#### **🟢 Tier 1: Very High Priority (Pure Math)**

1. **`P_Rand`** (16 calls/frame)
   - **What**: Returns random int
   - **Why**: Trivial; replace C `d_rand()` with OCaml `Random.int`
   - **Impact**: Reduces random-seeded divergences across 16+ call sites
   - **Porting effort**: Minimal (5 lines)

2. **`Calc_gun_point`** (~5 calls)
   - **What**: Computes firing position from gun mount relative to robot orientation
   - **Pure inputs**: Gun number, robot position, orientation (all provided)
   - **Why**: Pure 3D vector math on fixed-point integers
   - **Impact**: Eliminates divergence in gun point calculations (affects firing direction)
   - **Porting effort**: Low (~20 lines of vector math)

3. **`Make_random_vector`** (~5 calls)
   - **What**: Generates random normalized 3D vector
   - **Why**: Pure randomness; no world data needed
   - **Impact**: Used in AI decision branching; eliminating this effect would consolidate random paths
   - **Porting effort**: Low (~15 lines)

#### **🟡 Tier 2: Medium Priority (Potential Pure Logic)**

4. **`Move_towards_segment_center`** (6 calls)
   - **What**: Moves robot toward center of current segment
   - **Need**: Current segment geometry (provided in inputs)
   - **Why**: Likely pure vector subtraction/normalization
   - **Impact**: Reduces divergence in robot movement tracking
   - **Porting effort**: Medium (~30 lines)

5. **`Do_boss_stuff`** (2 calls)
   - **What**: Boss-specific state updates (teleporting, summons, etc.)
   - **Why**: May be isolated logic with limited side effects
   - **Impact**: Fixes boss AI behavior divergences
   - **Porting effort**: High (varies by boss implementation)

6. **`Do_ai_robot_hit_attack`** (3 calls)
   - **What**: Robot close-range collision attack (melee)
   - **Why**: Likely flag-setting or damage calculation
   - **Impact**: Fixes collision-based attack divergence
   - **Porting effort**: Medium (~15 lines)

#### **🔴 Tier 3: Low Priority (Architectural Blockers)**

7. **`Compute_vis_and_vec`** (~5 calls)
   - **Blocker**: Requires full FVI (Find Vector Intersection) via on-demand effects
   - **Current design**: Uses 3x effect callbacks (Fetch_segment_data, Fetch_object_data, Fetch_collision_table) per visibility check
   - **Inefficiency**: Shadow mode doubles computation (C runs FVI, then OCaml runs FVI via callbacks)
   - **Porting note**: FVI v2 is already ported; integrate it directly instead of effect-based
   - **Impact**: Would eliminate ~37-57 second headless replay slowdown

8. **Path creation effects** (`Create_path_to_player`, `Create_n_segment_path`, etc.)
   - **Blocker**: Full pathfinding requires traversing segment graph + obstacle avoidance
   - **Why**: Needs mutable world state (visited sets, path arrays) and segment connectivity
   - **Porting effort**: Very High (100+ lines per function)

9. **Multiplayer effects** (`Ai_multiplayer_awareness`, `Ai_multi_send_robot_position`)
   - **Why**: Network/game mode dependent; side effects on global state
   - **Porting effort**: High (complex game mode logic)

---

### RECOMMENDED PORTING SEQUENCE

To maximize reduction in `ai_local` divergence and improve headless replay performance:

1. **Start with `P_Rand` + `Make_random_vector`** (Week 1)
   - Replace C RNG calls; consolidates random branches
   - Test: Verify AI behavior determinism improves

2. **Port `Calc_gun_point`** (Week 1-2)
   - Pure 3D math
   - Test: Verify firing direction matches C across all robot types

3. **Port `Move_towards_segment_center`** (Week 2)
   - Validates segment position availability in inputs

4. **Consider full `Compute_vis_and_vec` integration** (Week 3+)
   - Merge FVI v2 directly into do_ai_frame instead of effect callbacks
   - Eliminates major shadow-mode performance bottleneck

---

### CURRENT EFFECT CALLSITES IN ox_ai_frame.ml

The file is split into three main functions:

- **`ai_do_actual_firing_stuff_d1`** (lines ~290-388): ~13 effects for D1 fire logic
- **`ai_do_actual_firing_stuff_d2`** (lines ~440-600): ~20 effects for D2 fire logic (with dual-gun, sniper, thief)
- **`do_ai_frame_d1`** (lines ~650-1500+): ~100 effects for full D1 AI frame
- **`do_ai_frame_d2`** (lines ~1700-2500+): ~160 effects for full D2 AI frame

The bridge adapter in `ai_frame_bridge.ml` (514 lines) handles all 23+ effects via `Effect.Deep.match_with`, dispatching to C externals and unpacking returned state.

---

### KEY OBSERVATION

**The biggest gains come from eliminating randomness-seeded divergences.** Currently, 16+ `P_Rand` calls per frame in do_ai_frame mean OCaml and C can follow completely different random paths after the first divergence. Porting `P_Rand` to use a shared PRNG state (or OCaml-authoritative randomness) would dramatically improve parity.

content.thinking:
`move_towards_segment_center` is relatively small but it calls `compute_segment_center`, `object_intersects_wall`, `move_object_to_legal_spot`, and `find_point_seg`. These are complex sub-functions that need world data. This isn't ideal for porting without those functions.

Let me reconsider. Let me look at how `move_towards_segment_center` is currently handled as an effect. It modifies `objp->pos` - so the effect needs to write back position changes. Let me see how the bridge handles it.

Actually, looking at this more carefully, `move_towards_segment_center` modifies the object position in C, and since OCaml is authoritative, the OCaml code would need to write back the position. But in the current setup, the effect fires, C modifies the position, and then OCaml's version of the position is stale.
 
So there's a fundamental conflict here: when OCaml runs do_ai_frame and triggers effects like Move_towards_segment_center, the C code modifies the object position, but then OCaml returns its own position values which overwrite whatever C just computed. This means the movement effect gets lost entirely.

The real question is whether do_ai_frame even returns position data at all, or if it's just returning other state changes. Let me trace through what the C bridge actually writes back to the object after OCaml's frame completes.

Looking at how the retry_count gets written back: OCaml calculates it as `(if !retry_count > 0 then !retry_count - 1 else 0)`, so if it had 2 retries internally, it returns 1, which then gets written to Ai_local_info since 1 > 0. C does the same calculation but with a different condition structure — it only writes retry_count if count > 0, then subtracts 1. The logic should be equivalent, but the divergence suggests OCaml's FVI collision detection or retry logic is behaving differently than C's.

`do_ai_robot_hit_attack` modifies `ailp->next_fire`, `GOAL_STATE`, and calls `collide_player_and_nasty_robot` and `set_next_fire_time`. These side effects would be hard to port because they depend on game functions like `collide_player_and_nasty_robot`.

Let me think about a better target. Let me reconsider the approach.

The BIGGEST categories of divergences:
1. AI local: 384783
2. Object: 247790
3. Global: 11829
4. Player: 6124

Many of these are cascading from random state divergence. 

The issue is that when OCaml calls effects into C, those C functions might invoke the random number generator internally, but the OCaml path could trigger different effects or call them in a different sequence depending on visibility and positioning. This causes the random state to desynchronize between the two implementations.

The real fix would be porting everything to OCaml to eliminate C callbacks entirely, but that's not practical. Instead, I should look for bugs in the existing OCaml code by comparing it carefully with the C version — particularly in functions like `set_next_fire_time` that get called from multiple places and could cascade divergences through `next_fire` and `next_fire2`.

let cur_segnum = ref obj_segnum in
    let cur_flags = ref obj_flags in
    let cur_phys_flags = ref phys_flags in
    let start_pos_x = pos_x in
    let start_pos_y = pos_y in
    let start_pos_z = pos_z in
    let orig_segnum = obj_segnum in
    let bounced = ref false in
    let obj_stopped = ref false in
    let retry_count = ref 0 in
    let n_ignore_objs = ref 0 in
    let ignore_objs = Array.create ~len:max_ignore_objs (-1) in
    let n_phys_segs = ref 0 in
    let phys_seglist = Array.create ~len:max_fvi_segs 0 in
    let try_again = ref true in
    let early_return = ref false in
    ignore phys_seglist;
    while !try_again && not !early_return do
      try_again := false;
      let fvx, fvy, fvz =
        Ox_math.vm_vec_copy_scale ~v:(!cur_vel_x, !cur_vel_y, !cur_vel_z) ~k:!sim_time
      in
      if fvx = 0 && fvy = 0 && fvz = 0
      then ()
      else (
        retry_count := !retry_count + 1;
        if !retry_count > 3 && not (obj_type = obj_player && !retry_count <= 8)
        then ()
        else (
        let new_px, new_py, new_pz =
          Ox_math.vm_vec_add ~a:(!cur_pos_x, !cur_pos_y, !cur_pos_z) ~b:(fvx, fvy, fvz)
        in
        let ignore_list = make_ignore_list ignore_objs !n_ignore_objs in
        let fq_flags = ref fq_check_objs in
        if obj_type = obj_weapon then fq_flags := !fq_flags lor fq_transpoint;
        if obj_type = obj_player then fq_flags := !fq_flags lor fq_get_seglist;
        let ( fate
            , hit_px
            , hit_py
            , hit_pz
            , hit_seg
            , hit_side
            , hit_side_seg
            , hit_objnum
            , wn_x
            , wn_y
            , wn_z
            , n_segs
            , seglist )
          =
          Effect.perform
            (Find_vector_intersection
               ( !cur_pos_x
               , !cur_pos_y
               , !cur_pos_z
               , new_px
               , new_py
               , new_pz
               , size
               , objnum
               , ignore_list
               , !fq_flags
               , !cur_segnum ))
        in
        (* D2: proximity + superprox mine hack — don't count as a retry *)
        if fate = hit_object && hit_objnum >= 0
        then (
          let (ho_type, ho_id) = Effect.perform (Query_object_type_id hit_objnum) in
          if ho_type = obj_weapon && (ho_id = proximity_id || ho_id = superprox_id)
          then retry_count := !retry_count - 1);
        (* Player seglist tracking *)
        if obj_type = obj_player
        then (
          if
            !n_phys_segs > 0
            && n_segs > 0
            && phys_seglist.(!n_phys_segs - 1) = seglist.(0)
          then n_phys_segs := !n_phys_segs - 1;
          let i = ref 0 in
          while !i < n_segs && !n_phys_segs < max_fvi_segs - 1 do

content.thinking:
So `compute_vis_and_vec` is still calling into C via an external. Let me check what the C implementation does.

Actually, from the MEMORY.md:> FVI Callback Registration - FVI data callbacks (Fetch_segment_data, Fetch_object_data, Fetch_collision_table) must be registered before use - `find_vector_intersection` in fvi.cpp registers them on first call- `compute_vis_and_vec` and `player_is_visible_from_object` in ai2.cpp/ai.cpp have `ensure_fvi_data_callbacks_registered()` for early registration


I see the issue now—when C's `compute_vis_and_vec` runs, it internally calls FVI, which in the OCaml build bridges back to OCaml's `find_vector_intersection` via `USE_OX_BRIDGE`. So there's a chain where OCaml calls C which calls back into OCaml's FVI, and that's where the performance bottleneck is coming from.

Rather than porting `compute_vis_and_vec` itself, I should focus on which functions would actually reduce divergences if moved to OCaml. The real divergence sources are collision/physics differences cascading into visibility and AI behavior, plus random number sequences diverging once any state differs. Let me look at `player_is_visible_from_object` since it's another visibility function called from `do_ai_frame` and might be a better candidate to examine.

Looking at the actual divergent fields in the logs, object 55's retry_count divergence at frame 16 comes from physics, not AI logic. Object 92's position and orientation bytes diverging at frame 43 suggests the physics engine itself is producing different collision results in OCaml. Without being able to determine the object types from the state logs alone, I need to trace through what `do_ai_frame` actually calls to find the real culprit.

Let me look at effects that modify AI state — specifically, effects that return data that OCaml uses to update ai_local fields.


Now I'm checking `Ai_follow_path` and `do_ai_robot_hit_attack` to see if they're sources of divergence. The hit attack effect is fire-and-forget, returning unit, so it doesn't feed state back to OCaml — the C side modifies `GOAL_STATE` and `next_fire` directly on the C object instead.

But here's the issue: OCaml writes back its own `goal_state` and `next_fire` at the end of `do_ai_frame`, which would overwrite whatever the C function changed. So I need to check whether OCaml actually knows that `goal_state` should be updated after calling this effect.

content.thinking:
OK, so `Do_ai_robot_hit_attack` in the AI frame (ox_ai_frame.ml line 142) is a unit effect — it just calls into C and comes back. The C function modifies `GOAL_STATE` and calls `set_next_fire_time` which modifies `next_fire`. But OCaml writes back its own goal_state and next_fire values.

Wait — but looking at the ai_frame_bridge code (lines 178-182), the C function `effect_do_ai_robot_hit_attack` modifies the C object directly. Then at line 750-781 in ai.cpp, OCaml's result overwrites those fields. 

So the C side's changes to GOAL_STATE and next_fire_time get completely lost when OCaml writes back its own values. This is definitely a divergence point — the C code sets GOAL_STATE to AIS_RECO and updates the fire time, but OCaml has no idea these happened and just overwrites them. The fix isn't to sync state back though; I need to port do_ai_robot_hit_attack to OCaml instead, which means I'll need to reimplement the collision detection, fire time logic, and whatever Robot_ fields it accesses.

Wait, actually set_next_fire_time is already ported as a bridge function, so that's not the blocker. The real issue is that there are two different versions of do_ai_robot_hit_attack — one for collisions and one for the AI frame loop — and I'm looking at the wrong one. Let me focus on the AI frame version in ox_ai_frame.ml where the C code sets GOAL_STATE and calls set_next_fire_time, but then OCaml returns its own result array that overwrites those changes.

Looking at the bridge handler, I need to find where the actual C implementation of the effect is being called and understand how the state modifications flow back.

content.thinking:
So the lambda calls `do_ai_robot_hit_attack(af_obj, ConsoleObject, &af_obj->pos)`. The C function modifies GOAL_STATE and next_fire on the object. But since OCaml's do_ai_frame returns its own GOAL_STATE and next_fire values, the C modifications are overwritten.

This is an expected divergence from an unported function. Porting `do_ai_robot_hit_attack` to OCaml would fix it, but the function calls `collide_player_and_nasty_robot` and `set_next_fire_time` which are complex.
 
Looking at what the function actually needs to do: check if firing is enabled and the player isn't dead, then if conditions are right (attack type 1, fire timer expired, player not cloaked and in range), trigger the collision damage and handle energy drain, set the goal state to recovery, and update the fire timer. The OCaml code already has access to all the necessary inputs to implement this logic.

The real problem is that after calling the C effect for the side effects, OCaml needs to also update its own goal_state and next_fire values locally, since the C function modifies those on its side but OCaml doesn't see those changes when it writes back its state.

Looking at the firing function, it seems like there's already a mechanism to read back the fire timer from the result array after the C function executes, so I need to trace through how that works and whether the same pattern applies to goal_state.

content.text: Let me look at how `ai_do_actual_firing_stuff` is called and what it returns to do_ai_frame.

content.content:
280→      ~goal_state
   281→      ~(goal_state_arr : int array)
   282→      ~current_gun
   283→      ~mode
   284→      ~behavior
   285→      ~next_fire
   286→      ~(achieved_state : int array)
   287→      ~player_visibility
   288→      ~object_animates
   289→      ~attack_type
   290→      ~n_guns
   291→      ~homing_flag
   292→      ~player_exploded
   293→      ~dist_to_player
   294→      ~obj_size
   295→      ~console_size
   296→      ~fvec_x
   297→      ~fvec_y
   298→      ~fvec_z
   299→      ~vtpx
   300→      ~vtpy
   301→      ~vtpz
   302→      ~gpx
   303→      ~gpy
   304→      ~gpz
   305→  =
   306→  let goal_state = ref goal_state in
   307→  let goal_state_arr = Array.copy goal_state_arr in
   308→  let current_gun = ref current_gun in
   309→  let mode = ref mode in
   310→  let next_fire = ref next_fire in
   311→  let advance_gun () =
   312→    current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
   313→    if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0
   314→  in
   315→  let fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz) =
   316→    let nf, _nf2, _rfc = Effect.perform (Ai_fire_laser_at_player (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz, !next_fire, 0)) in
   317→    next_fire := nf
   318→  in
   319→  if player_visibility = 2
   320→  then (
   321→    if (not (object_animates <> 0)) || !next_fire <= 0
   322→    then (
   323→      let dot =
   324→        Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(fvec_x, fvec_y, fvec_z) ~b:(vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
   325→      in
   326→      if dot >= 7 * f1_0 / 8
   327→      then (
   328→        if !current_gun < n_guns
   329→        then (

~goal_state
      ~(goal_state_arr : int array)
      ~current_gun
      ~mode
      ~behavior
      ~next_fire
      ~(achieved_state : int array)
      ~player_visibility
      ~object_animates
      ~attack_type
      ~n_guns
      ~homing_flag
      ~player_exploded
      ~dist_to_player
      ~obj_size

  =
  let goal_state = ref goal_state in
  let goal_state_arr = Array.copy goal_state_arr in
  let current_gun = ref current_gun in
  let mode = ref mode in
  let next_fire = ref next_fire in
  let advance_gun () =
    current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
    if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0
  in
  let fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz) =
    let nf, _nf2, _rfc = Effect.perform (Ai_fire_laser_at_player (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz, !next_fire, 0)) in
    next_fire := nf
  in
  if player_visibility = 2
  then (
    if (not (object_animates <> 0)) || !next_fire <= 0
    then (
      let dot =
        Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(fvec_x, fvec_y, fvec_z) ~b:(vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
      in
      if dot >= 7 * f1_0 / 8
      then (
        if !current_gun < n_guns
        then (
          if attack_type = 1
          then
            if player_exploded = 0 && dist_to_player < obj_size + console_size + (f1_0 * 2)
            then (
              let ok =
                Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness (robot_fire_agitation - 2))
              in
              if not ok then ();
              if ok then Effect.perform Do_ai_robot_hit_attack)
            else ()
          else if gpx = 0 && gpy = 0 && gpz = 0
          then ()
          else (
            let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
            if not ok then ();
            if ok
            then fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, 0, 0, 0, 0));
          if
            behavior <> aib_run_from
            && behavior <> aib_still
            && behavior <> aib_snipe
            && (!mode = aim_follow_path || !mode = aim_still)
          then mode := aim_chase_object);
        goal_state := ais_reco;
        goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
        advance_gun ())))
  else if homing_flag = 1
  then
    if
      ((not (object_animates <> 0)) || achieved_state.(!current_gun) = ais_fire)

content.thinking:
Now I can see the issue more clearly. In the OCaml D1 firing code:

Line 338: `if ok then Effect.perform Do_ai_robot_hit_attack` — this calls C which sets GOAL_STATE and calls set_next_fire_time
Line 353: `goal_state := ais_reco;` — OCaml sets goal_state to AIS_RECO (same as C)
Line 354: `goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;` — sets goal_state for current gun

But the issue is: C's `do_ai_robot_hit_attack` calls `set_next_fire_time` which modifies `next_fire`. OCaml doesn't update `next_fire` after `Do_ai_robot_hit_attack`. 

Looking at the C implementation, it checks if the attack type is 1 and next_fire is ready, then sets GOAL_STATE to AIS_RECO and calls `set_next_fire_time` to schedule the next shot — but OCaml only mirrors the GOAL_STATE change without updating the fire timing. So the C code has a nested condition check — it only calls `set_next_fire_time` when `next_fire <= 0`, meaning the cooldown has expired. The OCaml version might be missing this cooldown guard, which could explain why the firing behavior differs between the two implementations. I see the issue now — looking at the OCaml code structure more carefully, the `goal_state` assignment happens outside the entire attack type conditional block, so it gets executed regardless of which firing path was taken. The control flow nests the distance check inside the attack type check, which is different from the C version where those state changes were at a different scope level.

=
  let goal_state = ref goal_state in
  let goal_state_arr = Array.copy goal_state_arr in
  let current_gun = ref current_gun in
  let mode = ref mode in
  let next_fire = ref next_fire in
  let advance_gun () =
    current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
    if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0
  in
  let fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz) =
    let nf, _nf2, _rfc = Effect.perform (Ai_fire_laser_at_player (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz, !next_fire, 0)) in
    next_fire := nf
  in
  if player_visibility = 2
  then (
    if (not (object_animates <> 0)) || !next_fire <= 0
    then (
      let dot =
        Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(fvec_x, fvec_y, fvec_z) ~b:(vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
      in
      if dot >= 7 * f1_0 / 8
      then (
        if !current_gun < n_guns
        then (
          if attack_type = 1
          then
            if player_exploded = 0 && dist_to_player < obj_size + console_size + (f1_0 * 2)
            then (
              let ok =
                Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness (robot_fire_agitation - 2))
              in
              if not ok then ();
              if ok then Effect.perform Do_ai_robot_hit_attack)
            else ()
          else if gpx = 0 && gpy = 0 && gpz = 0
          then ()
          else (
            let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
            if not ok then ();
            if ok
            then fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, 0, 0, 0, 0));
          if
            behavior <> aib_run_from
            && behavior <> aib_still
            && behavior <> aib_snipe
            && (!mode = aim_follow_path || !mode = aim_still)
          then mode := aim_chase_object);
        goal_state := ais_reco;
        goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
        advance_gun ())))
  else if homing_flag = 1
  then
    if
      ((not (object_animates <> 0)) || achieved_state.(!current_gun) = ais_fire)
      && !next_fire <= 0
    then (
      let hit_dist = Effect.perform (Compute_vis_and_vec (0, 0, 0)) in
      let _, _, _, _, hit_far_enough = hit_dist in
      if hit_far_enough <> 0
      then (
        let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
        if ok
        then (
          fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, 0, 0, 0, 0);
          goal_state := ais_reco;
          goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
          advance_gun ())
        else advance_gun ())
      else advance_gun ())
    else advance_gun ();
  [| !goal_state
   ; goal_state_arr.(0)
   ; goal_state_arr.(1)
   ; goal_state_arr.(2)
   ; goal_state_arr.(3)
   ; goal_state_arr.(4)
   ; goal_state_arr.(5)
   ; goal_state_arr.(6)
   ; goal_state_arr.(7)
   ; !current_gun
   ; !mode
   ; !next_fire
  |]
;;

        if attack_type = 1        then            if ... then (mp_awareness; hit_attack)            else ()        else if gpx = 0 ... then ()        else (mp_awareness; fire_laser);
        (* lines 347-352: mode change *)        if behavior <> aib_run_from ... then mode := aim_chase_object);
    (* lines 353-355: ALWAYS if dot >= 7/8 *)
    goal_state := ais_reco;
    goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
    advance_gun ())
```

In C, the same condition triggers weapon firing logic, but there's an early return when multiplayer awareness fails, which means the subsequent state updates and gun advancement don't execute in that case. Still writing control flow...

Actually, I need to reconsider case 3 — when the gun point is zero in C, it also proceeds through the mode check and goal state update, so that's not actually a bug. The real issues are the three cases where OCaml fails to return early: when attack_type is 1 but distance is insufficient, when attack_type is 1 with distance but multiplayer awareness is false, and when attack_type isn't 1 but multiplayer awareness is false despite a valid gun point. I'm tracing through the control flow more carefully now—the mode change happens inside the gun check block, but then the goal state updates and gun counter increments happen outside of it. Let me verify the exact nesting by looking at the indentation again to understand where each block closes. I need to figure out whether the goal state assignment and gun counter increment happen inside or outside the current gun check. Looking back at the original C code, it seems like those operations should be inside the loop that iterates through guns, so they execute for each weapon the robot has. Tracing through the brace structure more carefully now—the outer `if (dot >= ...)` block wraps everything, with nested conditionals for the gun check, attack type validation, and player state. The else branches handle alternative paths, and after all that nesting closes out around line 3110, there's the goal state assignment and gun advancement logic before the final closing brace at 3119.

Looking at the OCaml equivalent, it mirrors this exact structure: the dot threshold check contains the current gun conditional, which contains the firing logic, and then outside that inner check but still inside the dot check, goal_state gets updated and the gun advances. The key difference is OCaml has no early returns—the firing code just produces unit values, so execution naturally flows through to the goal state updates.

This means the OCaml version has a bug: when the C code would return early (for attack type 1 at distance, or when multiplayer awareness is false), the OCaml code instead continues and still updates goal_state and advances the gun. Since this is D1 code and the parity test might be checking D2 instead, I should look at the D2 firing function to see if the same issue exists there.

    let nf, nf2, _rfc = Effect.perform (Ai_fire_laser_at_player (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz, !next_fire, !next_fire2)) in
    next_fire := nf;
    next_fire2 := nf2;
    last_fired_updated := 1
  in
  let do_ranged_fire ~fire_px ~fire_py ~fire_pz =
    if gpx = 0 && gpy = 0 && gpz = 0
    then ()
    else (
      let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
      if ok
      then
        if gun_num <> 0
        then (
          if !next_fire <= 0
          then
            fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz);
          if !next_fire2 <= 0 && weapon_type2 <> -1
          then (
            let gp2x, gp2y, gp2z = Effect.perform (Calc_gun_point 0) in
            fire_laser (gp2x, gp2y, gp2z, 0, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz)))
        else if !next_fire <= 0
        then
          fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz))
  in
  if player_visibility = 2 || dist_to_last_fired_upon < fire_at_nearby_threshold
  then (
    (* Path 1: visible or recently fired upon *)
    (* fire_pos = Believed_player_pos by default;
       if not melee and player not directly visible, use Last_fired_upon_player_pos.
       We approximate Last_fired_upon_player_pos with believed_player_pos since they
       are usually very close (Last is set to believed after each fire). *)
    let fire_px = ref believed_px in
    let fire_py = ref believed_py in
    let fire_pz = ref believed_pz in
    if
      (not (object_animates <> 0))
      || !next_fire <= 0
      || (!next_fire2 <= 0 && weapon_type2 <> -1)
    then (
      let dot =
        Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(fvec_x, fvec_y, fvec_z) ~b:(vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
      in
      if dot >= 7 * f1_0 / 8 || (dot > f1_0 / 4 && boss_flag <> 0)
      then (
        if gun_num < n_guns
        then (
          if attack_type = 1
          then (
            if player_exploded = 0 && dist_to_player < obj_size + console_size + (f1_0 * 2)
            then (
              let ok =
                Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness (robot_fire_agitation - 2))
              in
              if ok then Effect.perform Do_ai_robot_hit_attack))
          else do_ranged_fire ~fire_px:!fire_px ~fire_py:!fire_py ~fire_pz:!fire_pz;
          if
            behavior <> aib_run_from
            && behavior <> aib_still
            && behavior <> aib_snipe
            && behavior <> aib_follow
            && attack_type = 0
            && (!mode = aim_follow_path || !mode = aim_still)
          then mode := aim_chase_object);
        goal_state := ais_reco;
        goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
        advance_gun ())))
  else if attack_type = 0 && (homing_flag = 1 || (weapon_type2 <> -1 && homing_flag2 = 1))
  then
    if
      (* Path 2: homing weapons *)
      ((not (object_animates <> 0)) || achieved_state.(!current_gun) = ais_fire)
      && ((!next_fire <= 0 && !current_gun <> 0) || (!next_fire2 <= 0 && !current_gun = 0))
    then (
      (* Check Hit_pos distance > 40*F1_0 via effect *)
      let _, _, _, _, hit_far_enough = Effect.perform (Compute_vis_and_vec (0, 0, 0)) in

          current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
          if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0))
      else (
        current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
        if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0))
    else (
      current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
      if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0)
  else (

I found the bug. In C's `ai_do_actual_firing_stuff`, when `ai_multiplayer_awareness` returns false or when `attack_type==1` but the robot is too far, C does `return` — exiting the entire function and **skipping** `GOAL_STATE = AIS_RECO` and gun advance. But in OCaml, these cases are `()` no-ops that **fall through** to always set `goal_state` and advance the gun.

            fire_laser (gp2x, gp2y, gp2z, 0, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz)))
        else if !next_fire <= 0
        then
          fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz))
  in

      else false (* C returns: skip goal_state/advance *))

(* Pure game logic for AI frame processing.
   ai_do_actual_firing_stuff + do_ai_frame (D1 + D2).
   All side effects are performed via algebraic effects. *)

let f1_0 = 0x10000

(* -- Constants ------------------------------------------------------------ *)

(* AI animation states *)
let ais_none = 0
let ais_rest = 1
let ais_srch = 2
let ais_lock = 3
let ais_flin = 4
let ais_fire = 5
let ais_reco = 6
let ais_err_ = 7

(* AI modes *)
let aim_still = 0
let aim_wander = 1
let aim_follow_path = 2
let aim_chase_object = 3
let aim_run_from_object = 4
let aim_behind = 5            (* D2: replaces D1's AIM_HIDE *)
let aim_follow_path_2 = 6
let aim_open_door = 7
let aim_goto_player = 8
let aim_goto_object = 9
let aim_snipe_attack = 10
let aim_snipe_fire = 11
let aim_snipe_retreat = 12
let aim_snipe_retreat_backwards = 13
let aim_snipe_wait = 14
let aim_thief_attack = 15
let aim_thief_retreat = 16
let aim_thief_wait = 17

(* AI behaviors *)
let aib_still = 0x80
let aib_normal = 0x81
let aib_behind = 0x82
let aib_run_from = 0x83
let aib_snipe = 0x84
let aib_station = 0x85
let aib_follow = 0x86

let min_behavior = aib_still
let max_behavior_d1 = aib_station
let max_behavior_d2 = aib_follow

(* Player awareness types *)
let pa_nearby_robot_fired = 1
let pa_weapon_wall_collision = 2
let pa_player_collision = 3
let pa_weapon_robot_collision = 4
let robot_brain = 7
let ndl = 5
let robot_fire_agitation = 94
let gm_multi = 38
let segment_is_robotmaker = 2
let chase_time_length = f1_0 * 8
let ri_cloaked_except_firing = 1
let player_flags_cloaked = 0x800
let player_flags_headlight_on = 0x4000
let pf_uses_thrust = 0x40

(* D2-only constants *)
let snipe_retreat_time = f1_0 * 5
let snipe_abort_retreat_time = snipe_retreat_time / 2
let snipe_attack_time = f1_0 * 10
let snipe_wait_time = f1_0 * 5
let snipe_fire_time = f1_0 * 2
let thief_attack_time = f1_0 * 10
let thief_wait_times = [| f1_0 * 30; f1_0 * 25; f1_0 * 20; f1_0 * 15; f1_0 * 10 |]
let sub_flags_sprox = 0x02
let sub_flags_camera_awake = 4
let proximity_id = 47 (* D1 *)
let proximity_id_d2 = 47 (* D2 *)
let superprox_id_d2 = 53

(* AI transition table (from ai.h / ai.cpp):
   [awareness_type-1][current_state][goal_state] -> new_goal_state
   Matches Ai_transition_table in main_d2/ai.cpp exactly.
   C table is [4][7][7]; OCaml extends to [4][8][8] for AIS_ERR_ entries. *)
let ai_transition_table =
  [| (* AIE_FIRE: PA_NEARBY_ROBOT_FIRED - 1 = 0 *)
     [| (* current = AIS_NONE *)
        [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; (* current = AIS_REST *)
        [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; (* current = AIS_SRCH *)
        [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; (* current = AIS_LOCK *)
        [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; (* current = AIS_FLIN *)
        [| ais_err_; ais_rest; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; (* current = AIS_FIRE *)
        [| ais_err_; ais_fire; ais_fire; ais_fire; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; (* current = AIS_RECO *)
        [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_fire; ais_err_ |]
      ; (* current = AIS_ERR_ *)
        [| ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_err_ |]
     |]
   ; (* AIE_HITT: PA_WEAPON_WALL_COLLISION - 1 = 1 *)
     [| [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_rest; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_fire; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_err_ |]
     |]
   ; (* AIE_COLL: PA_PLAYER_COLLISION - 1 = 2 *)
     [| [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_rest; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_fire; ais_reco; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_lock; ais_flin; ais_fire; ais_fire; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_err_ |]
     |]
   ; (* AIE_HURT: PA_WEAPON_ROBOT_COLLISION - 1 = 3 *)
     [| [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_err_; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_flin; ais_err_ |]
      ; [| ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_rest; ais_err_ |]
     |]
  |]
;;

(* -- Algebraic effects ---------------------------------------------------- *)

type _ Effect.t +=
  | Ai_multiplayer_awareness : int -> bool Effect.t
  | Do_ai_robot_hit_attack : unit Effect.t
  | Ai_fire_laser_at_player : (int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int) -> (int * int * int) Effect.t
  | Calc_gun_point : int -> (int * int * int) Effect.t
  | Create_path_to_player : (int * int) -> int array Effect.t
  | Create_path_to_station : int -> int array Effect.t
  | Create_n_segment_path : (int * int) -> int array Effect.t
  | Create_n_segment_path_to_door : (int * int) -> int array Effect.t
  | Attempt_to_resume_path : int array Effect.t
  | Ai_follow_path : (int * int * int * int * int) -> int array Effect.t
  | Move_towards_segment_center : unit Effect.t
  | Compute_vis_and_vec : (int * int * int) -> (int * int * int * int * int) Effect.t
  | Ai_multi_send_robot_position : int -> unit Effect.t
  | Do_boss_stuff : int -> unit Effect.t
  | P_Rand : int Effect.t
  | Make_random_vector : (int * int * int) Effect.t
  | Object_to_object_visibility : int Effect.t
  | Do_snipe_frame : (int * int * int * int * int * int) -> int array Effect.t  (* returns path state *)
  | Do_escort_frame : (int * int) -> int array Effect.t  (* returns path state *)
  | Do_thief_frame : (int * int * int * int * int * int * int) -> int array Effect.t  (* returns path state *)
  | Do_any_robot_dying_frame : bool Effect.t
  | Make_nearby_robot_snipe : unit Effect.t
  | Move_away_from_player : unit Effect.t
  | Openable_doors_in_segment : int Effect.t  (* returns side index or -1 *)
  | Invalidate_escort_goal : unit Effect.t
  | Laser_create_new_easy :
      (int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int)
      -> unit Effect.t
  (* Do_companion_extras: danger laser evasion + flare firing for companion robot.
     Args: (dist_to_player, player_visibility, vtpx, vtpy, vtpz, mode, next_fire, next_fire2)
     Returns: updated next_fire *)
  | Do_companion_extras : (int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int) -> int Effect.t
  (* Do_thief_extras: flare firing for thief robot.
     Args: (dist_to_player, player_visibility, vtpx, vtpy, vtpz, next_fire, next_fire2)
     Returns: updated next_fire *)
  | Do_thief_extras : (int * int * int * int * int * int * int) -> int Effect.t

(* Ai_fire_laser_at_player args: gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz
   D1: gun_num=0, fire_pos=(0,0,0) ignored on C side
   D2: gun_num=actual, fire_pos=Believed_player_pos or Last_fired_upon_player_pos *)

(* Compute_vis_and_vec args: vis_vec_pos(3)
   Returns: (player_visibility, vtpx, vtpy, vtpz, sound_flag) *)

(* Ai_follow_path args: (player_vis, prev_vis, vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
   D1: only uses first 2 args; D2: all 5 *)

(* Laser_create_new_easy args: (fvx, fvy, fvz, fpx, fpy, fpz, objnum, weapon_id) *)

(* -- AI state packed array layout ----------------------------------------- *)
(* Input indices for ai_state packed array *)
let idx_skip_ai_count = 0
let idx_goal_state = 1
let idx_current_state = 2

let idx_danger_laser_num = 10
let idx_danger_laser_sig = 11

(* ailp fields *)
let idx_next_fire = 14
let idx_next_fire2 = 15

let idx_player_awareness_time = 17
let idx_mode = 18
let idx_time_since_processed = 19
let idx_consecutive_retries = 20
let idx_retry_count = 21

(* goal_state[8] *)

(* time_player_seen *)
let idx_time_player_seen = 30

(* goal_segment *)

(* rapidfire_count *)

(* achieved_state[8] *)

(* previous_visibility *)

(* D2: next_action_time *)

(* Total AI state packed array size *)

(* Robot info packed array layout *)
let ri_attack_type = 0

let ri_circle_distance = 19 (* array of NDL=5 *)
let ri_turn_time = 24 (* array of NDL=5 *)
let ri_pursuit = 29

(* -- maybe_ai_do_actual_firing_stuff -------------------------------------- *)

let maybe_ai_do_actual_firing_stuff ~game_mode ~goal_state ~obj_id ~current_state =
  if
    game_mode land gm_multi <> 0
    && goal_state <> ais_flin
    && obj_id <> robot_brain
    && current_state = ais_fire
  then true
  else false
;;

(* -- ai_do_actual_firing_stuff D1 ----------------------------------------- *)

let ai_do_actual_firing_stuff_d1
      ~goal_state
      ~(goal_state_arr : int array)
      ~current_gun
      ~mode
      ~behavior
      ~next_fire
      ~(achieved_state : int array)
      ~player_visibility
      ~object_animates
      ~attack_type
      ~n_guns
      ~homing_flag
      ~player_exploded
      ~dist_to_player
      ~obj_size
      ~console_size
      ~fvec_x
      ~fvec_y
      ~fvec_z
      ~vtpx
      ~vtpy
      ~vtpz
      ~gpx
      ~gpy
      ~gpz
  =
  let goal_state = ref goal_state in
  let goal_state_arr = Array.copy goal_state_arr in
  let current_gun = ref current_gun in
  let mode = ref mode in
  let next_fire = ref next_fire in
  let advance_gun () =
    current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
    if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0
  in
  let fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz) =
    let nf, _nf2, _rfc = Effect.perform (Ai_fire_laser_at_player (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz, !next_fire, 0)) in
    next_fire := nf
  in
  if player_visibility = 2
  then (
    if (not (object_animates <> 0)) || !next_fire <= 0
    then (
      let dot =
        Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(fvec_x, fvec_y, fvec_z) ~b:(vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
      in
      if dot >= 7 * f1_0 / 8
      then (
        if !current_gun < n_guns
        then (
          if attack_type = 1
          then
            if player_exploded = 0 && dist_to_player < obj_size + console_size + (f1_0 * 2)
            then (
              let ok =
                Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness (robot_fire_agitation - 2))
              in
              if not ok then ();
              if ok then Effect.perform Do_ai_robot_hit_attack)
            else ()
          else if gpx = 0 && gpy = 0 && gpz = 0
          then ()
          else (
            let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
            if not ok then ();
            if ok
            then fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, 0, 0, 0, 0));
          if
            behavior <> aib_run_from
            && behavior <> aib_still
            && behavior <> aib_snipe
            && (!mode = aim_follow_path || !mode = aim_still)
          then mode := aim_chase_object);
        goal_state := ais_reco;
        goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
        advance_gun ())))
  else if homing_flag = 1
  then
    if
      ((not (object_animates <> 0)) || achieved_state.(!current_gun) = ais_fire)
      && !next_fire <= 0
    then (
      let hit_dist = Effect.perform (Compute_vis_and_vec (0, 0, 0)) in
      let _, _, _, _, hit_far_enough = hit_dist in
      if hit_far_enough <> 0
      then (
        let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
        if ok
        then (
          fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, 0, 0, 0, 0);
          goal_state := ais_reco;
          goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
          advance_gun ())
        else advance_gun ())
      else advance_gun ())
    else advance_gun ();
  [| !goal_state
   ; goal_state_arr.(0)
   ; goal_state_arr.(1)
   ; goal_state_arr.(2)
   ; goal_state_arr.(3)
   ; goal_state_arr.(4)
   ; goal_state_arr.(5)
   ; goal_state_arr.(6)
   ; goal_state_arr.(7)
   ; !current_gun
   ; !mode
   ; !next_fire
  |]
;;

(* -- ai_do_actual_firing_stuff D2 ----------------------------------------- *)

let ai_do_actual_firing_stuff_d2
      ~goal_state
      ~(goal_state_arr : int array)
      ~current_gun
      ~mode
      ~behavior
      ~next_fire
      ~next_fire2
      ~(achieved_state : int array)
      ~player_visibility
      ~object_animates
      ~attack_type
      ~n_guns
      ~weapon_type2
      ~homing_flag
      ~homing_flag2
      ~boss_flag
      ~player_exploded
      ~dist_to_player
      ~obj_size
      ~console_size
      ~fvec_x
      ~fvec_y
      ~fvec_z
      ~vtpx
      ~vtpy
      ~vtpz
      ~gpx
      ~gpy
      ~gpz
      ~gun_num
      ~dist_to_last_fired_upon
      ~fire_at_nearby_threshold
      ~difficulty_level
      ~frame_time
      ~believed_px
      ~believed_py
      ~believed_pz
  =
  let goal_state = ref goal_state in
  let goal_state_arr = Array.copy goal_state_arr in
  let current_gun = ref current_gun in
  let mode = ref mode in
  let last_fired_updated = ref 0 in
  let next_fire = ref next_fire in
  let next_fire2 = ref next_fire2 in
  let advance_gun () =
    current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
    if !current_gun >= n_guns
    then if n_guns = 1 || weapon_type2 = -1 then current_gun := 0 else current_gun := 1
  in
  let fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz) =
    let nf, nf2, _rfc = Effect.perform (Ai_fire_laser_at_player (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fpx, fpy, fpz, !next_fire, !next_fire2)) in
    next_fire := nf;
    next_fire2 := nf2;
    last_fired_updated := 1
  in
  let do_ranged_fire ~fire_px ~fire_py ~fire_pz =
    if gpx = 0 && gpy = 0 && gpz = 0
    then ()
    else (
      let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
      if ok
      then
        if gun_num <> 0
        then (
          if !next_fire <= 0
          then
            fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz);
          if !next_fire2 <= 0 && weapon_type2 <> -1
          then (
            let gp2x, gp2y, gp2z = Effect.perform (Calc_gun_point 0) in
            fire_laser (gp2x, gp2y, gp2z, 0, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz)))
        else if !next_fire <= 0
        then
          fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, fire_px, fire_py, fire_pz))
  in
  if player_visibility = 2 || dist_to_last_fired_upon < fire_at_nearby_threshold
  then (
    (* Path 1: visible or recently fired upon *)
    (* fire_pos = Believed_player_pos by default;
       if not melee and player not directly visible, use Last_fired_upon_player_pos.
       We approximate Last_fired_upon_player_pos with believed_player_pos since they
       are usually very close (Last is set to believed after each fire). *)
    let fire_px = ref believed_px in
    let fire_py = ref believed_py in
    let fire_pz = ref believed_pz in
    if
      (not (object_animates <> 0))
      || !next_fire <= 0
      || (!next_fire2 <= 0 && weapon_type2 <> -1)
    then (
      let dot =
        Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(fvec_x, fvec_y, fvec_z) ~b:(vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
      in
      if dot >= 7 * f1_0 / 8 || (dot > f1_0 / 4 && boss_flag <> 0)
      then (
        if gun_num < n_guns
        then (
          if attack_type = 1
          then (
            if player_exploded = 0 && dist_to_player < obj_size + console_size + (f1_0 * 2)
            then (
              let ok =
                Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness (robot_fire_agitation - 2))
              in
              if ok then Effect.perform Do_ai_robot_hit_attack))
          else do_ranged_fire ~fire_px:!fire_px ~fire_py:!fire_py ~fire_pz:!fire_pz;
          if
            behavior <> aib_run_from
            && behavior <> aib_still
            && behavior <> aib_snipe
            && behavior <> aib_follow
            && attack_type = 0
            && (!mode = aim_follow_path || !mode = aim_still)
          then mode := aim_chase_object);
        goal_state := ais_reco;
        goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
        advance_gun ())))
  else if attack_type = 0 && (homing_flag = 1 || (weapon_type2 <> -1 && homing_flag2 = 1))
  then
    if
      (* Path 2: homing weapons *)
      ((not (object_animates <> 0)) || achieved_state.(!current_gun) = ais_fire)
      && ((!next_fire <= 0 && !current_gun <> 0) || (!next_fire2 <= 0 && !current_gun = 0))
    then (
      (* Check Hit_pos distance > 40*F1_0 via effect *)
      let _, _, _, _, hit_far_enough = Effect.perform (Compute_vis_and_vec (0, 0, 0)) in
      if hit_far_enough <> 0
      then (
        let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
        if ok
        then (
          fire_laser (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, believed_px, believed_py, believed_pz);
          goal_state := ais_reco;
          goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
          (* Path 2 gun advance: C always wraps to 0, unlike advance_gun which wraps to 1 for dual-weapon robots *)
          current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
          if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0)
        else (
          current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
          if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0))
      else (
        current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
        if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0))
    else (
      current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
      if !current_gun >= n_guns then current_gun := 0)
  else (
    (* Path 3: blind fire at recently-seen position *)
    let prand = Effect.perform P_Rand in
    if prand / 2 < Ox_math.fixmul ~a:frame_time ~b:((difficulty_level lsl 12) + 0x4000)
    then
      if dist_to_last_fired_upon < fire_at_nearby_threshold
      then
        if
          (not (object_animates <> 0))
          || !next_fire <= 0
          || (!next_fire2 <= 0 && weapon_type2 <> -1)
        then (
          (* Direction computed on C side as vec to Last_fired_upon_player_pos *)
          let dot =
            Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(fvec_x, fvec_y, fvec_z) ~b:(vtpx, vtpy, vtpz)
          in
          if dot >= 7 * f1_0 / 8
          then (
            if !current_gun < n_guns
            then (
              if attack_type = 1
              then (
                if
                  player_exploded = 0
                  && dist_to_player < obj_size + console_size + (f1_0 * 2)
                then (
                  let ok =
                    Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness (robot_fire_agitation - 2))
                  in
                  if ok then Effect.perform Do_ai_robot_hit_attack))
              else if gpx = 0 && gpy = 0 && gpz = 0
              then ()
              else (
                let ok = Effect.perform (Ai_multiplayer_awareness robot_fire_agitation) in
                if ok
                then
                  if gun_num <> 0
                  then (
                    if !next_fire <= 0
                    then
                      fire_laser
                        (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, believed_px, believed_py, believed_pz);
                    if !next_fire2 <= 0 && weapon_type2 <> -1
                    then (
                      let gp2x, gp2y, gp2z = Effect.perform (Calc_gun_point 0) in
                      fire_laser
                        (gp2x, gp2y, gp2z, 0, believed_px, believed_py, believed_pz)))
                  else if !next_fire <= 0
                  then
                    fire_laser
                      (gpx, gpy, gpz, gun_num, believed_px, believed_py, believed_pz));
              if
                behavior <> aib_run_from
                && behavior <> aib_still
                && behavior <> aib_snipe
                && behavior <> aib_follow
                && (!mode = aim_follow_path || !mode = aim_still)
              then mode := aim_chase_object);
            goal_state := ais_reco;
            goal_state_arr.(!current_gun) <- ais_reco;
            let () =
              current_gun := !current_gun + 1;
              if !current_gun >= n_guns
              then if n_guns = 1 then current_gun := 0 else current_gun := 1
            in
            ())
          else advance_gun ())
        else advance_gun ());
  [| !goal_state
   ; goal_state_arr.(0)
   ; goal_state_arr.(1)
   ; goal_state_arr.(2)
   ; goal_state_arr.(3)
   ; goal_state_arr.(4)
   ; goal_state_arr.(5)
   ; goal_state_arr.(6)
   ; goal_state_arr.(7)
   ; !current_gun
   ; !mode
   ; !last_fired_updated
   ; !next_fire
   ; !next_fire2
  |]
;;

(* -- do_ai_frame D1 ------------------------------------------------------- *)

let do_ai_frame_d1
      ~(ai_state : int array)
      ~(rinfo : int array)
      ~frame_time
      ~frame_count
      ~game_time
      ~game_mode
      ~difficulty_level
      ~overall_agitation
      ~player_is_dead
      ~player_exploded
      ~player_flags
      ~obj_x
      ~obj_y
      ~obj_z
      ~obj_segnum
      ~obj_size
      ~obj_id
      ~objnum
      ~console_x
      ~console_y
      ~console_z
      ~console_size
      ~believed_x
      ~believed_y
      ~believed_z
      ~believed_seg
      ~(orient : int array)
      ~(gun_point_in : int array)
      ~seg_special
      ~(cloak_last_pos : int array)
      ~cloak_last_time
      ~ai_evaded_in
  =
  (* Unpack AI state into refs *)
  let skip_ai_count = ref ai_state.(idx_skip_ai_count) in
  let goal_state = ref ai_state.(idx_goal_state) in
  let current_state = ref ai_state.(idx_current_state) in
  let cloaked = ref ai_state.(idx_cloaked) in
  let current_gun = ref ai_state.(idx_current_gun) in
  let cur_path_index = ref ai_state.(idx_cur_path_index) in
  let behavior = ref ai_state.(idx_behavior) in
  let hide_index = ref ai_state.(idx_hide_index) in
  let path_length = ref ai_state.(idx_path_length) in
  let _sub_flags = ai_state.(idx_sub_flags) in
  let goalside = ref ai_state.(idx_goalside) in
  let hide_segment = ref ai_state.(idx_hide_segment) in
  let next_fire = ref ai_state.(idx_next_fire) in
  let _next_fire2 = ref ai_state.(idx_next_fire2) in
  let player_awareness_type = ref ai_state.(idx_player_awareness_type) in
  let player_awareness_time = ref ai_state.(idx_player_awareness_time) in
  let mode = ref ai_state.(idx_mode) in
  let time_since_processed = ref ai_state.(idx_time_since_processed) in
  let consecutive_retries = ref ai_state.(idx_consecutive_retries) in
  let retry_count = ref ai_state.(idx_retry_count) in
  let goal_state_arr = Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i -> ai_state.(idx_goal_state_arr + i)) in
